texts.full_text: artnered with a D.C.-linked public relations firm to fight the boycott, divestment and sanctions (BDS) movement, according to leaked emails obtained by Mondoweiss. The PR firm 30 Point Strategies has discussed how to combat BDS at the University of California, Los Angeles campus with Hillel staffers there. 30 Point staffer Michael Fletcher told the Jewish Journal that they have been hired “to assist UCLA Hillel in developing a communications strategy for its public affairs and marketing efforts.” The collaboration comes as part of an increasing effort by pro-Israel groups to push back against BDS at university campuses. In May, The Times of Israel reported that StandWithUs has launched similar campaigns in more than 10 American universities under the title “Students Against Anti-Semitism,” with a focus On countering Palestinian narratives and advocating for divestment from companies profiting off human r

texts.full_text: o the micro-morphology which is due to phase separation of thThe study on proton conductivity in Nafion 112 membranes using electrochemical atomic force microscopy (EC-AFM) reveals several key points: ### Key Findings: - Objective: The primary goal was to measure and analyze the spatial distribution of proton conductivity with high resolution, identifying factors influencing this property. - Methodology: EC-AFM was employed due to its ability for detailed imaging beyond conventional methods. - Results: - Proton conductivity showed an uneven (inhomogeneous) distribution across the membrane. - This inhomogeneity is attributed to limited connectivity between hydrophilic domains, which are crucial for proton conduction as they remain hydrated and conductive. Inadequate connections lead to inconsistent pathways for protons. - Underlying Cause: The observed micro-morphology results from phase separation during manufacturing or operation, where polymer chains (hydrophobic) separate from ion-exchange groups (more hydrophilic), creating regions of varying conductivity. ### Limitations Identified: 1. Tip Effects in EC-AFM: - Measurement inaccuracies due to the influence of the AFM probe's shape and proximity on readings at micro scales. 2. Temporal Resolution Constraints: - The technique struggles with capturing dynamic changes quickly, making it unsuitable for real-time monitoring under rapidly changing fuel cell conditions. 3. Local vs Bulk Information: - EC-AFM provides localized data but lacks comprehensive insights into bulk conducti

texts.full_text: f the holiday display. Eccentric Visions: The Worlds of Luo Ping (1733-1799) - Metropolitan Museum of Art Through January 10, 2010 - Luo Ping was one of the most versatile, original, and celebrated artists in eighteenth-century China. The youngest of the so-called Eight Eccentrics of Yangzhou, he was a fiercely independent artist whose works -- including portraits, landscapes, and flower paintings -- deeply influenced the course of later Chinese painting. Organized by the Museum Rietberg, Zurich, the exhibition, which consists of 37 paintings by Luo Ping, members of his family, and his mentor Jin Nong, is drawn primarily from leading museums in China and will feature a number of National Treasures that have never been shown in the West. In New York it will be complemented by about a dozen works from the Museum's collection and from several local private lenders. Watteau to Degas: French Drawings from the Frits Lugt Collection - Frick Collection Through January 10, 2010 - Selected by the curators of The Frick Collection, this exhibition of more than sixty works will include drawings and watercolors by well-known masters of the French School, including Jean-Antoine Watteau, François Boucher, Jean-Honoré Fragonard, Jacques-Louis David, Eugène Delacroix, Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres, and Edgar Degas, as well as important figures who are less familiar to the general public. Art of the Samurai: Japanese Arms and Armor, 1156-1868 - Metropolitan Museum of Art Through January 10, 2010 - The first comprehensive exhibition devoted to the arts of the samurai. Arms and armor will be the principal focus, bringing together the finest examples of armor, swords and sword mountings, archery equipment and firearms, equestrian equipment, banners, surcoats, and related accessories of rank such as fans and batons. Drawn entirely from public and private collections in Japan, the majority of objects date from the rise of the samurai in the late Heian period, ca. 1156, through the early modern Edo period, endin
